title: The Macro Minute: Are investors too complacent about Wednesday's reciprocal tariff deadline?

macro minute for Monday July 7th 2025.

weekend. So as always we'll start with
the executive summary from today's
leadoff morning note. If you would like
to review the analysis supporting these
conclusions and to see if you need to
make any changes in your portfolio to
remain on the right side of market risk
or to get on the right side of market
risk to the extent you have been
following KISS and Dr. Mo need to be a
client of Freddy Tacro. So today's
Kimacro question is, are investors too
complacent about Wednesday's reciprocal
tariff deadline? Our answer, no. Akin to
our signaling regarding congressional
brinksmanship last week, investors
should continue to treat trade policy
uncertainty as one of several scarecrows
to be faded by every investor with the
time horizon that extends past this
summer. For our February 5th fleet off
morning note, all told, we do not
believe tariffs are a mere negotiating
tactic. Investors should however
anticipate more successful negotiations
and for some of those negotiations to
end in more weak trade deals that
achieve very little in support of
restoring manufacturing jobs or reducing
US trade deficits. President Trump is
unlikely to pass up any opportunity to
claim victory. However, however shallow
and largely symbolic such as the US MCA
and the phase 1 China trade deal which
he negotiated during his first term for
our April 12th around the horn
presentation. In our view, this past
week represents the most
transformational week in asset market
since the Lehman Brothers bankruptcy
catalyzed nearly two decades of fiscal
monetary policy large s geopolitically
driven stress in the treasury market is
accelerating the transition from US
exceptionalism to the US being an
emerging market in capital flight risk
terms. President Trump may be operating
under the assumption that the US is too
big to fail and foreign creditors may
check upon that. Markets thought Lehman
was too big to fail too. This transition
has several profound implications for
asset markets. Number one, the rest of
the world has much more negotiating
leverage than anyone in the Trump
administration is willing to admit
publicly, increasing the probability
that reciprocal tariff rates are broadly
lowered in deals that President Trump
will celebrate as wins, but are likely
to be less effective than the phase one
China trade deal and US MCA that he
negotiated during his first term. We
wouldn't be here today if the art of the
deal actually worked back then.

obviously in the America again clients
thousands of clients 80 plus countries
around the world. Not everyone has 401ks
and IAS in country A, country B, country

specifically for Americans. Obviously,

or IRA, you're going to have tax
advantages that, you know, allow you to
execute this and any other uh uh risk
management overlay uh to your benefit
from a tax perspective. But even in even
if you are executing KISS in a taxable
brokerage account, it's still way better
than not executing KISS in a tax broker.
back taxable brokerage account. You
know, the alternative to not, you know,
employing institutional grade risk
management overlays to your portfolio is
having your portfolio crash. Would you
rather have your portfolio crash and not
pay taxes or would you rather book gains
ahead of the crash because you have
institutional grade riskmanagement
overlays like the ones we feature in our
systematic portfolio construction
process. Would you rather book the gains
ahead of the crash and buy the dip so
that you can eliminate the volatility
drag from your portfolio or a large
degree of it which helps you compress
the amount of time it takes you to grow
your account to a desirable level so
that you can retire on time and
comfortably. That's what institutional
grade risk management overlays do in
terms of adding volatility targeting and
dynamic position sizing to your account.
If you choose to ignore those things,
i.e. You don't want KISS, you want to
keep doing what you've been doing, which
is, hey, every two to three years, by by
the way, that's that's now reality. Go
back to 2018. We've had three market
crashes. So, no, we've had four market
crashes since Q4 2018. Q4 2018, Q1 of
2020, uh, obviously the first three

another one in Q1 of 2025. So, we've had
four market crashes since Q4 of 2018.
Four. And every single one of those
times, if you have been working for 20,
30, 40, you know, years to accumulate
the amount of money that's in your
account that you've been saving for
retirement or saving to to give your
children uh leave them a gift uh after
you go to the great beyond. If you've
been working your whole life to save
that money, I got to imagine it's not
going to feel very good to be down 20%
from your high water mark at any given
time. And so this is what KISS avoids.
It it it adds risk management to
investors in a way that allows them to
continue participating in in in bull
markets as they get closer and closer to
retirement. Most high pie chart asset
allocations and target date funds. They
want you to get out of stock and Bitcoin
as you get closer to retirement. And
they want to give you bonds which very
clearly we're in a bond a structural
bond bear market. I mean I don't know
how you can argue that we're not at this
point. Uh we're in a structural bond
bear market and Wall Street wants to
stuff you into a bunch of bonds as you
get older. In my opinion, that that may
be smart, at least it was smart when we
were in a 40-year bond bull market. But
in my opinion, I think the number one
asset class in the world that has the
maximum amount of risk over the medium
to long term is the Treasury bond
market. So why in the world would you
want to increasingly allocate your life
savings to an asset to what I could
consider to be based on our research,
which I think is some of the best in the
world, to a melting ice cube of an asset
class. To me, I think it's a much
smarter choice is to belong the asset
classes that are going to go up at
exponential rates while also having the
peace of mind and the and the and the
and the confidence to remain invested
because, you know, the the risk
management overlays that we feature in
KISS are going to help you, you know,
raise cash and get to the sidelines
either early in or right before a big
big decline or a big crash in the
markets that again allow you to
eliminate all that stress and baggage
from your portfolio and life. by the
way, uh can't imagine uh being married
to somebody whose portfolio is down 25%
from the highs. That can't seem very
pleasant, especially if you've been
working for 20 30 years to accumulate
that life savings. I can imagine that
might end up in divorce. So again, this
is there's so many benefits to our Kiss
strategy. I'm going to stop rambling,
but the number one thing I will say is
if you're concerned about taxes and the
average right now, Kisses averages two
trades per month. If you're concerned
about taxes on those two trades per
month, just know that your choice to
hodddle and not pay taxes, huddle and
not pay taxes will result in you your
portfolio being down 20 to 30% from the
highs every two to three years. That
sounds crazy to me if that's your
choice. I'd rather pay taxes to avoid
that. But again, you also don't have to
execute kiss in a taxable brokerage
account. The most thoughtful among our
clients are the ones executing it in tax
advantage accounts. And so there's so

can't win with KISS, it's on you, not
